mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflects broad economic momentum. This strength translates into higher expected corporate earnings and consumer spending, driving positive equity returns over the medium term.
evidence_refs: fact:Strong labor markets are historically correlated with economic expansions.
failure_condition: The 3-month change in PAYEMS shows a statistically significant negative correlation with S&P 500 20-day forward returns over any 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds rate rises above a restrictive level, borrowing costs for corporations and consumers increase, slowing economic activity. This compression of multiples and earnings expectations leads to negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:The 2006-2007 tightening cycle preceded the 2008 financial crisis and market decline.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 50% of instances when the FEDFUNDS rate is above 4.0%.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous rise in the unemployment rate and a fall in inflation (disinflation) signals a sharp drop in aggregate demand, characteristic of an impending recession. This combination is bearish for equities as it points to declining corporate revenues and pricing power.
evidence_refs: fact:The Sahm Rule uses a similar unemployment-based trigger to identify recessions.
failure_condition: The combined signal does not precede negative 60-day S&P 500 returns in at least 60% of triggered cases over a full business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: During a post-recession recovery, accelerating retail sales signal a rapid rebound in consumer spending. This robust demand drives corporate revenue growth and leads to positive short-term equity performance as the economy exits a trough.
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending represents approximately two-thirds of U.S. GDP.
failure_condition: The rank correlation between the 3-month change in RSAFS and 20-day forward returns is below 0.05 during periods identified as post-recession recoveries (e.g., 2009-2010, 2020-2021).

mechanism_narrative: A sharp contraction in industrial production is a classic leading indicator of a broad economic slowdown. This weakness in the goods-producing sector signals falling business investment and demand, leading to negative equity market returns.
evidence_refs: fact:NBER recession dating criteria include significant declines in industrial production.
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces positive 60-day returns in more than 50% of instances following a trigger event over the last three business cycles.

mechanism_narrative: An unexpectedly high monthly inflation print forces the market to price in a more aggressive central bank response. This increases the discount rate applied to future earnings and creates policy uncertainty, causing a negative short-term reaction in equities.
evidence_refs: fact:Market reactions to CPI releases during the 2022-2023 period were strongly negative on upside surprises.
failure_condition: The average 5-day S&P 500 return following a trigger event is not statistically significantly negative (t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: A sustained decline in housing starts is a powerful leading indicator of economic slowdown, as the housing sector is highly sensitive to interest rates and consumer confidence. This weakness precedes broader economic contraction and negative equity returns.
candidate_trigger: HOUST 6-month moving average falls below 12-month moving average
evidence_refs: fact:Significant downturns in housing starts preceded the 2001 and 2008 recessions.
failure_condition: The signal's walk-forward information coefficient (IC) is not consistently negative (i.e., IC > -0.02) over rolling 3-year periods.

mechanism_narrative: The combination of slowing real GDP growth and high, persistent inflation creates a stagflationary environment. This is highly toxic for equities, as corporate profit margins are compressed by rising input costs while revenue growth falters, leading to poor earnings and negative returns.
candidate_trigger: GDPC1 year-over-year % change < 1.5% AND CPIAUCSL year-over-year % change > 4.0%
evidence_refs: fact:The stagflation of the 1970s was a prolonged bear market for U.S. equities.
failure_condition: The average 60-day forward return for the S&P 500 is positive during periods that meet the trigger condition.
